title: Impairment — Two-Step Test
event: Hollis Co.'s equipment has a carrying value of $600,000. Expected future undiscounted net cash flows are $580,000, and the equipment's fair value is $520,000. Prepare the required entry.

entries.label: Test with undiscounted CF, measure with FV: loss 80,000

lines.label: 600,000 CV − 520,000 FV
lines.why: Step 1 (recoverability): undiscounted 580,000 < carrying 600,000 → impaired. Step 2 (measurement): write down to FAIR VALUE.
lines.trap: Two different yardsticks on purpose — test with undiscounted cash flows, MEASURE with fair value. Mixing them (a 20,000 loss) is the planted exam error.

lines.label: carrying value reduced
lines.why: The write-down runs through the contra-asset; carrying value becomes 520,000.
lines.trap: The 520,000 is the new depreciable base going forward — and held-for-use impairments are NEVER restored.

memorize.body: Step 1: impaired if undiscounted future cash flows < carrying value. Step 2: loss = carrying value − FAIR value.
If undiscounted flows had been 620,000, NO loss — even with FV at 520,000. Failing step 1 ends the analysis.

questions.prompt: CV $600,000, undiscounted CF $580,000, FV $520,000. Impairment loss = ?

distractors.feedback: You measured against the undiscounted 580,000. That figure only TESTS; the loss measures against FAIR VALUE: 600,000 − 520,000 = 80,000.
Step 1 fails recoverability (580 < 600), so a loss IS required — measured to fair value: 80,000.

build.scaffold: Two steps, two yardsticks: undiscounted flows decide IF, fair value decides HOW MUCH.
